items.q: Does a stack remove the item that has been waiting longest?
items.correct: No - a stack is LIFO: pop removes the most recently pushed item from the top; it is a queue that removes the longest-waiting item (FIFO).
items.misconception: Yes - all data structures release their oldest item first.
items.random_wrong: A stack removes a random item each time.
items.why: Stack and queue behaviours are easily swapped. Anchor the metaphors: a stack of plates (take the top one) versus a queue of people (serve the front).

items.q: Does a collision mean a hash table has failed?
items.correct: No - collisions, where two keys hash to the same index, are expected; techniques such as chaining or probing handle them while keeping fast average performance.
items.misconception: Yes - a collision corrupts the table and loses one of the items.
items.random_wrong: Yes - hash functions are designed so collisions can never occur.
items.why: Students treat the hash function as magic and unique. With more keys than slots, collisions are mathematically inevitable; good design manages them rather than preventing them.

items.q: Does an in-order traversal of any binary tree produce sorted output?
items.correct: No - in-order traversal is only guaranteed to give ascending order for a binary SEARCH tree, where the left-less-than-right ordering property holds.
items.misconception: Yes - in-order traversal sorts whatever tree it is applied to.
items.random_wrong: Yes - all tree traversals output items in sorted order.
items.why: The traversal gets credit that belongs to the tree's structure. In-order just means left-root-right; without the BST ordering invariant, the output order is arbitrary.

items.q: Which data structure operates on a last-in-first-out basis?
items.why: It's Stack. “Queue” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The advantage of a circular queue over a simple linear array queue is that it:
items.correct: Reuses the array space freed at the front when items are dequeued
items.misconception: Stores items in sorted order automatically
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reuses the array space freed at the front when items are dequeued. “Stores items in sorted order automatically” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A hash table achieves fast lookup by:
items.correct: Applying a hash function to the key to compute its storage index directly
items.misconception: Keeping all keys in sorted order
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Applying a hash function to the key to compute its storage index directly. “Keeping all keys in sorted order” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree visits the items in:
items.why: It's Ascending order. “Random order” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A queue receives 8 enqueue operations and then 3 dequeue operations. How many items remain in the queue?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 items. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
